title: Income Insurance and the Equilibrium Term Structure of Equity
abstract: Output, wages, and dividends feature term structures of variance ratios that are respectively flat, increasing, and decreasing. Income insurance from shareholders to workers explains these term structures. Risk-sharing smooths wages but only concerns transitory risk and hence enhances short-run dividend risk. As a result, actual labor-share variation largely forecasts the risk, premium, and slope of dividend strips. A simple general equilibrium model in which labor rigidity affects dividend dynamics and the price of short-run risk reconciles standard asset pricing facts with the term structures of the equity premium, volatility, and macroeconomic variables, which are at odds in leading models.

article.introduction:
Leading asset pricing modelsdescribe many characteristics of financial markets but fail to explain the timing of equity risk. These models have different rationales (e.g., habit formation, time-varying expected growth, disasters, and prospect theory), but share an important feature: priced risk comes from variation in long-run discounted cash flows.1In contrast, van Binsbergen, Brandt, and Koijen (2012), van Binsbergen et al. (2013), and van Binsbergen and Koijen (2017) document that the term structures of equity volatility and premia are downward-sloping, that is, markets compensate short-run risk. Moreover, standard models are usually based on assumptions concerning dividend dynamics that imply an upward-sloping term structure of dividend risk (i.e., volatilities or variance ratios (VRs) of dividends' growth rates, which increase with the horizon). Instead, and consistent with Belo, Collin-Dufresne, and Goldstein (2015), this paper documents that dividend risk is strongly downward-sloping, which implies that many models overestimate long-horizon dividend risk by an order of magnitude.
Why is dividend risk downward-sloping? Under which conditions does downward-sloping dividend risk transmit to equity risk and premia? And what macroeconomic channel explains short-term equity returns? This paper empirically and theoretically addresses these questions by providing a macroeconomic foundation of the timing of risk and by reconciling, in equilibrium, standard asset pricing facts with new evidence about term structures. This is important because the term structures of both fundamentals and equity provide information about how prices are determined in equilibrium. Hence, a term structure perspective offers additional testable implications for asset pricing frameworks and can help us understand the macroeconomic determinants of asset prices.
In this paper, I argue that labor rigidity is at the heart of the timing of macroeconomic risk. Danthine and Donaldson (1992,2002), among others, show that a mechanism of income insurance from shareholders to workers, which takes place within the firm, leads to volatile and procyclical dividends. This mechanism explains why equity commands high compensation.2Beyond such acyclicality effectof income insurance, however, I show that aterm structure effectalso takes place. Since output, wages, and dividends are cointegrated (Lettau and Ludvigson (2005)), income insurance implies that the transitory component of aggregate risk is shared asymmetrically between workers and shareholders, whereas the permanent component is faced by both. In particular, wages are partially insured with respect to transitory risk, whereas dividends load more on transitory risk as a result of operating leverage. Thus, wage (dividend) risks shift toward the long (short) horizon, and workers (shareholders) bear more long-run (short-run) risk. I embed this mechanism of income insurance in an otherwise standard closed-form general equilibrium model and show that, under standard preferences, the term structure effect of income insurance is inherited by financial markets, leading to downward-sloping term structures of equity risk and premia. As a result, actual labor-share variation largely predicts short-term equity risk, premium, and slope.
Empirical analysis supports the main model mechanism. First, I document that the timing of risk is heterogeneous across macroeconomic variables. The term structures of risk of output, wage, and dividend risk are, respectively, flat, increasing, and decreasing. In line with the model, these term structures—and the cointegrated relationships among the levels of these variables—support the idea that while workers and shareholders are both subject to permanent shocks, they share transitory shocks asymmetrically as a result of income insurance. Similarly, Gamber (1988) and Menzio (2005) show that implicit contracts and labor market search frictions lead to wage rigidity over transitory risk only, Guiso, Pistaferri, and Schivardi (2005) and Ellul, Pagano, and Schivardi (2014) provide evidence that insurance does not concern permanent shocks, and Ríos-Rull and Santaeulàlia-Llopis (2010) document that the wage-share is stationary and countercyclical.
Second, I find further support for the term structure effect of income insurance by investigating the model predictions that the VRs of dividends should be decreasing with the labor-share and the gap between the VRs of wages and dividends should be increasing with the labor-share. The model predicts that, after a negative transitory shock, wages are partially insured, whereas dividends are more exposed. This implies that wages are high relative to dividends (the cyclicality effect) and that wages load less than dividends on transitory risk (the term structure effect). Therefore, the distance between the upward-sloping VRs of wages and the downward-sloping VRs of dividends should increase when the labor-share is high. The opposite holds after a positive transitory shock. I provide robust empirical evidence that such a relation obtains in the data.
Third, I document a strong connection between labor-share variation and short-term equity returns. The correlations between the labor-share and one-year-ahead dividend strip volatility (risk), excess return over the risk-free rate (premium), and excess return over the market return (slope) are 86%, 57%, and 52%, respectively.
Fourth, I show that the remainder of output minus wages features a term structure of risk that is markedly downward-sloping and essentially recovers the negative slope of dividend risk. This implies that the bulk of the gap between the approximatively flat VRs of output and the decreasing VRs of dividends should be imputed to labor. Consistent with this idea, I document that the transitory component of dividends that should not be imputed to income insurance does not help explain the negative slope of dividend risk or dividend strip returns. Finally, I find that the term structure effect of income insurance implies that the labor-share positively forecasts dividend and consumption growth, which I show to be a robust feature of the data.
The model consists of a few simple ingredients and is similar to Greenwald, Lettau, and Ludvigson (2014). Investment is not directly modeled and total resources are shared by workers and shareholders. The former do not invest but rather consume their wages (Berk and Walden (2013)), while the latter receive dividends and thus act as a representative agent in the financial markets. Total resources are subject to both permanent and transitory technological shocks (Bansal, Kiku, and Yaron (2010), Lettau and Ludvigson (2014)).3Income insurance concerns transitory risk, as suggested by the empirical analysis. The insurance mechanism generates countercyclical labor-share and downward-sloping dividend risk, on the one hand, but enhances the pricing of short-run risk, on the other hand. Indeed, the equilibrium state-price density equals shareholders' marginal utility but is affected by income insurance through the levered dynamics of dividends. Unlike Danthine and Donaldson (2002), the model leads to a stationary equilibrium even in the presence of permanent shocks (time-varying long-run growth as in Bansal and Yaron (2004)) and recursive preferences. Under standard preferences (i.e., intertemporal substitution larger than the wealth effect and a preference for the early resolution of uncertainty), the term structure of equity premia is nonmonotone: long-run growth leads to an upward-sloping effect and transitory risk to a downward-sloping effect. Transitory shocks are expected to recover and hence are risky in the short run and safe in the long run. For income insurance strong enough, operating leverage enhances the transitory risk of dividends and the equilibrium price for this risk. In turn, the negative slope of dividend risk transmits to equity returns.
The model calibration exploits the information contained in the term structures of aggregate consumption, wage, and dividend risk. On the one hand, these term structures represent additional stylized facts captured by the model. On the other hand, the timing of macroeconomic risk allows one to quantitatively measure the strength of income insurance and hence operating leverage on dividends. The term structures of risk also provide information about the persistence of latent factors—a main issue in asset pricing models. The baseline calibration reconciles the term structures of both fundamentals and equity with the traditional asset pricing facts, such as the risk-free rate and equity premium puzzles as well as the excess volatility of equity (also at long horizons, as in Beeler and Campbell (2012)). In line with the data, the slope of equity premia is a function of both the cash flow and the discount rate channels. Moreover, the model leads to upward-sloping real interest rates. van Binsbergen et al. (2013) and Aït-Sahalia, Karaman, and Mancini (2015) refine the findings of van Binsbergen, Brandt, and Koijen (2012), suggesting that the negative slope of equity premia is an unconditional property of the data, whereas conditional equity premia are slightly increasing in good times and strongly decreasing in bad times. Such dynamics obtain in the model—without negatively impacting other results—once fundamentals feature business cycle uncertainty. Time-varying risk premia lead to a pattern of long-horizon predictability consistent with the data. Most general equilibrium asset pricing models—such as the long-run risk model of Bansal and Yaron (2004), which obtains as a subcase—cannot explain all of these stylized facts at the same time. Closed-form solutions allow one to decompose the equity premium and derive an equilibrium density that captures the relative contribution of discounted cash flows at each horizon. In the baseline calibration, the horizon that contributes most to the equity premium is the immediate future, that is, the density is monotone decreasing (it is hump-shaped in a long-run risk model). This suggests that a “short-run” explanation of financial markets is needed to reconcile the timing of macroeconomic risk with the traditional asset pricing facts.
Several recent papers suggest alternative and complementary channels that can help explain the term structure of equity. See van Binsbergen and Koijen (2017) for a review.4
The economic mechanism in Belo, Collin-Dufresne, and Goldstein (2015) differs from that of this paper. Belo, Collin-Dufresne, and Goldstein (2015) argue that the negative slope of dividend risk comes from rigidity in financial leverage: financial leverage transforms upward-sloping Earnings before interest and taxes (EBIT) risk into downward-sloping dividend risk. In contrast, I empirically show that the term structure of EBIT risk is markedly downward-sloping and close to that of dividends, and thus the financial leverage channel can have only a limited term structure effect. Most of the change in the slope obtains earlier than financing decisions and should be imputed to wages. Croce, Lettau, and Ludvigson (2015) study investors' beliefs about fundamentals. They assume the usual long-run risk dynamics for dividends and show that bounded rationality under limited information can lead to downward-sloping dividend risk under the subjective measure of investors. While this mechanism is complementary to that in the current paper, Croce, Lettau, and Ludvigson (2015) assume that dividend risk is upward-sloping under the physical measure. Dividend risk is markedly downward-sloping, however, in actual data. As a result, the beliefs formation channel can have only a limited term structure effect relative to the term structure effect driven by wages. Ai et al. (2015) propose a general equilibrium model whereby heterogeneous investment risk due to vintage capital leads to a downward-sloping term structure of equity premia. The investment channel is potentially complementary to the income insurance mechanism. However, the term structure of risk of output minus wages almost recovers the negative slope of dividend risk. Moreover, the term structure of risk of output minus investments is almost identical to that of output. As a result, investment does not induce a downward-sloping effect. Both Belo, Collin-Dufresne, and Goldstein (2015) and Kogan and Papanikolaou (2013) are partial equilibrium models: given dividends, a downward-sloping term structure of equity premia obtains because of the exogenous correlations between the priced factors and dividend risk in those economies. A general equilibrium approach would produce economic restrictions on those correlations and offer additional insights into the relationship between either financial leverage or investment-specific risk and equity term structures. Hasler and Marfè (2016) suggest that the negative slope of dividend risk is due to postdisaster recovery. As long as dividends recover after being hit by a disaster, dividend risk shifts toward the short horizon. While this mechanism is both alternative and complementary to that of income insurance, the two interact with each other since human capital is usually less affected by disasters than physical capital.
A number of recent papers highlight the importance of labor relations for asset pricing. This paper complements this literature by investigating the term structure implications of income insurance in general equilibrium for both dividends and equity. Note that labor market frictions—for example, infrequent wage resettling in Favilukis and Lin (2015), search frictions in Kuehn, Petrosky-Nadeau, and Zhang (2012), and labor mobility in Donangelo (2014)—can lead to a similar term structure effect. I model income insurance as an empirically motivated risk-sharing rule to capture in reduced form the combinations of labor market mechanisms and frictions that give rise to labor rigidity. While Danthine and Donaldson (1992,2002), Favilukis and Lin (2015), and Kuehn, Petrosky-Nadeau, and Zhang (2012) propose big macrofinance models and rely on numerical solutions, I consider a simpler and more parsimonious economy and provide analytical solutions.5Recently, Greenwald, Lettau, and Ludvigson (2014) investigate the asset pricing implications of low-frequency swings in labor-share.
The model also has implications for the cross section of equity returns. Lettau and Wachter (2007,2011) show in partial equilibrium that a model that captures the downward-sloping term structure of equity automatically gives rise to a value premium, when value firms are defined as having shorter duration equity than growth firms. The term structure effect of income insurance provides a foundation for the intuition of Lettau and Wachter (2007). Indeed, Marfè (2015a) shows that such a result obtains in an extended version of the model and provides empirical support by documenting a strong relation between variation in the labor-share and value premium dynamics. Thus, the model is also consistent with Koijen, Lusting, and van Nieuwerburgh (2014), who empirically document that markets compensate short-run risk and that value premium dynamics are related to business cycle risk.
The model emphasis on the fundamentals' transitory risk is key to understanding the term structure of equity. However, the model has some limitations in the description of real interest rates. In particular, the model qualitatively captures the positive sign of the slope and the long-run real yield, but it quantitatively overestimates the level of the curve.6This can be explained by the extreme form of limited market participation assumed in the model. Since workers do not access financial markets, the state-price density inherits the large transitory risk of dividends, and bonds are essentially hedge instruments against dividend risk. This contrasts with the results of Alvarez and Jermann (2005), who infer a prominent role of permanent risk in discount rates.7Such model tension could be relaxed in a framework that endogenizes participation in stock and bond markets, in the spirit of Berk and Walden (2013).
The paper is organized as follows. SectionI.provides empirical support for the main model assumptions, mechanism, and predictions. SectionII.describes the economy and derives the equilibrium asset pricing predictions. SectionIII.presents asset pricing results. A model extension is discussed in SectionIV., and SectionV.concludes.
article.I. Empirical Analysis:
The first part of this section documents that the timing of risk is heterogeneous among macroeconomic variables. The second part argues and empirically supports the idea that a mechanism of income insurance between workers and shareholders is at the heart of the timing of risk. The third part shows that income insurance is a main driver of the risk and premia of dividend strips. The final part provides robustness tests. The remainder of the paper shows that an otherwise standard general equilibrium model with income insurance reconciles standard asset pricing facts with the new evidence about the term structure of equity.
I employ postwar data from the U.S. nonfinancial corporate sector (such as value-added, labor compensations, EBIT, and dividends from the Flow of Funds).
I define the timing of risk of a given variable as the term structure of its VRs (that is, the ratio of the growth rate variance at horizon τ relative to τ times the variance at horizon one). VRs capture whether the variance of a given variable increases linearly with the observation interval. Hence, downward-sloping VRs below unity imply that risk concentrates at short horizons, while upward-sloping VRs above unity imply that risk concentrates at long horizons.
The left panel of Figure1provides a number of insights. As can be seen, the VRs of wages are markedly upward-sloping and above unity, the VRs of EBIT and dividends are markedly downward-sloping and below unity, and the VRs of value added lie in the middle and are approximatively flat. These results are consistent with and extend those of Beeler and Campbell (2012) and Belo, Collin-Dufresne, and Goldstein (2015). Note that asset pricing models' usual assumptions about the dynamics of dividends are strongly inconsistent with the data. In particular, time variation in the conditional moments of a geometric process (e.g., long-run growth, stochastic volatility, and time-varying disasters) induces markedly upward-sloping VRs. Thus, long-horizon dividend risk is usually overestimated by an order of magnitude or more.8
What explains the heterogeneity in the timing of macroeconomic risk? I argue that the main driver is an explicit or implicit mechanism of income insurance from shareholders to workers that takes place within the firm. The rationale is as follows.
On the one hand, Danthine and Donaldson (2002), among others, suggest that risk-sharing between workers and shareholders (as well as labor market frictions) leads to smooth wages and, as a result of operating leverage, risky dividends. Hence, the labor-share is countercyclical and the dividend share is procyclical, as documented by Ríos-Rull and Santaeulàlia-Llopis (2010). I refer to this mechanism as the cyclicality effect of income insurance.
On the other hand, Guiso, Pistaferri, and Schivardi (2005) document that while such an income insurance mechanism exists, it does not concern permanent shocks. Menzio (2005) shows theoretically and empirically that labor market frictions lead to wage rigidity with respect to transitory shocks instead of permanent shocks. Further, Lettau and Ludvigson (2014) document that macroeconomic variables are subject to both permanent and transitory shocks and Lettau and Ludvigson (2005) document that wages and dividends are cointegrated, that is, subject to the same permanent shocks. I extend this result by providing evidence that all quantities in Figure1are cointegrated (see Internet Appendix SectionI.).
The above suggests that, beyond the cyclicality effect of income insurance, a term structure effect also takes place. Given cointegration, the income insurance mechanism concerns only transitory shocks. Thus, after a negative transitory shock, the fraction of total resources devoted to workers' remuneration is partially insured by a decrease in the resources devoted to shareholders' remuneration. Such an insurance mechanism implies that the labor-share (dividend-share) is decreasing (increasing) with the transitory component of output. As a consequence, wages (dividends) load on transitory risk less (more) than output. This produces VRs of wages (dividends) that are increasing (decreasing) and above (below) the VRs of output. Such a term structure effect of income insurance coincides with the empirical VRs in the left panel of Figure1.
The right panel of Figure1provides strong empirical support for the term structure effect of income insurance and its magnitude. Indeed, the VRs of the remainder of value added minus wages recover the negative slope of the term structures of both EBIT and dividends. This means that wages explain the bulk of the distance between the flat term structure of value added and the downward-sloping term structure of dividends. In particular, the increasing (decreasing) VRs of wages (dividends) represent the other side of the coin of wage rigidity (operational leverage on dividends). Instead, alternative channels such as investment (suggested by Ai et al. (2015) and Kogan and Papanikolaou (2013)) or financial leverage (suggested by Belo, Collin-Dufresne, and Goldstein (2015)) have little or negligible impact on the timing of dividend risk.9In the following, I provide more formal empirical support for the cyclicality and term structure effects of income insurance.
Based on the discussion above, the cyclicality effect of income insurance should imply that (i) changes in the labor-share are negatively correlated with changes in output and (ii) the labor-share is negatively correlated with the dividend-share.
Consistent with a number of previous studies, I find empirical support for both of these stylized facts. The labor-share is countercyclical—the correlation between changes in the labor-share and changes in log value added is −40.2%—and moves negatively with the dividend-share—the correlation between labor- and dividend-shares is −23.5%. These results are in line with the empirical findings of Boldrin and Horvath (1995), Shimer (2005), and Ríos-Rull and Santaeulàlia-Llopis (2010). Internet Appendix SectionI.documents that the labor-share Granger causes the dividend-share, whereas investment and financing decisions do not.
If income insurance concerns only transitory shocks, then wages (dividends) should load less (more) than output on transitory risk. Given cointegration, this leads to a term structure effect. In particular, after a negative (positive) transitory shock, workers' remuneration increases (decreases) relative to total resources, and the VRs of wages and dividends increase and decrease (decrease and increase), respectively, relative to those of output.
The sign and significance of the coefficients provide strong support for the term structure effect of income insurance. When resources devoted to workers' remuneration are high (low) relative to total resources, dividends load more (less) on transitory risk and their VRs decrease (increase). Indeed,bdis negative and highly significant at all horizons τ considered. The opposite holds for wages: the relation between their VRs and the labor-share is positive andbwis significant for τ large enough.11
In turn, a positive and highly significant relation obtains between the labor-share and the distance between the VRs of wages and dividends (i.e.,bwd>0). Note that the sign and significance of the coefficients hold when controlling for financing and investment decisions. The adjusted-R2s are similar too.12
The left panel of Figure2shows the positive relationship between the labor-share and the gap between the VRs of wages and dividends. The middle and the right panels show the positive and negative relationships between the labor-share and the VRs of wages and dividends, respectively. The above results strongly support the idea that income insurance within the firm is a main driver of the timing of dividend risk.
I now shift focus from macroeconomic fundamentals to the term structure of equity. As long as income insurance induces a term structure effect, I expect variation in the labor-share to be related to the risk and return on short-term assets, such as dividend strips. This could obtain by means of both the cash flow channel and the discount rate channel.
Since income insurance induces a leverage effect on dividends and enhances their short-run risk, we expect a positive relation between the labor-share and the three dependent variables.
TableIIreports the regression results. The labor-share significantly and positively predicts dividend strip volatility one quarter, one year, and two years ahead. Newey and West (1987)t-statistics are 5.88, 6.03, and 5.37, and the adjusted-R2s are extremely high at 72%, 74%, and 62% (without controls), respectively. Note that these variables do not share a time trend and are not strongly persistent (the half-life of labor-share is about 3.5 years).14
The left panel of Figure3illustrates how labor-share shapes dividend strip volatility: both time series increase between 1996 and 2001, decrease up to 2007, and then increase again up to 2010. To shed light on how the explanatory power of labor-share relates to other potential drivers of equity risk, Panel A of TableIIIreports horse race regressions. I consider the following controls: the three Fama and French (1993) factors (excess market return, SMB return, and HML return), market return volatility, log price-earnings ratio, risk-free rate, cay, as well as financial leverage and investment-share from the nonfinancial corporate sector.
The labor-share coefficients remain highly significant with the inclusion of all of these controls. Moreover, the horse race regressions do not change the positive sign and magnitude of the labor-share coefficients, and the adjusted-R2s do not increase substantially in any of the horse race regressions. Taken together, the results suggest that labor-share variation is an important driver of short-term equity risk.15
TableIIalso shows that the labor-share significantly and positively predicts dividend strip returns in excess of the risk-free rate and the market return. In the former case, Newey and West (1987)t-statistics and adjusted-R2s are 1.99 and 8% (one quarter ahead), 3.02 and 31% (one year ahead), and 6.26 and 64% (two years ahead). In the latter case, Newey and West (1987)t-statistics and adjusted-R2s are 1.98 and 10% (one quarter ahead), 2.58 and 26% (one year ahead), and 2.43 and 28% (two years ahead). The time series are plotted in middle and right panels of Figure3. Similar to the case of volatility, Panels B and C of TableIIIpresent horse race regressions with the same set of controls. Again, the labor-share coefficients remain positive and significant in all of the horse race regressions and the adjusted-R2s do not increase substantially.16
Overall, the above analysis strongly suggests that labor-share variation is an important driver not only of short-term equity risk but also of the short-term equity premium and slope. Income insurance therefore seems to be more informative about short-term equity returns than many financial indicators (e.g., valuation ratios and Fama and French (1993) factors) and alternative macroeconomic channels (e.g., financial and investment decisions).
The previous analysis documents that income insurance affects the term structure of both dividend risk and equity returns. I now look at additional testable implications. As long as income insurance concerns transitory risk only, the labor-share should positively forecast dividend growth. This obtains because, on the one hand, the labor-share is a decreasing function of the transitory component of output and, on the other hand, dividends positively load on such a transitory shock. I therefore perform long-horizon predictability regressions of dividend growth rates on the labor-share. Panel A of TableIVreports the estimation results.
The regressions provide additional support for the income insurance mechanism. The coefficients are positive and highly significant (as usual, Newey and West (1987) and Hansen and Hodrick (1980)t-statistics are used to account for autocorrelation and heteroskedasticity of residuals as well as overlapping samples). The explanatory power is large with adjusted-R2s ranging from about 0% to 37%. The income insurance mechanism also implies that the labor-share should positively forecast consumption growth. Panel B of TableIVprovides evidence that this is the case. The coefficients are positive and significant at all of the horizons considered and the adjusted-R2s range from about 6% to 36%. These results provide further support for the income insurance mechanism, which leads to dividend and consumption predictability.17
This section aims to verify whether downward-sloping dividend risk as well as short-term equity risk, premium, and slope can be imputed to sources of transitory dividend risk distinct from income insurance.
First, I isolate the transitory component of dividends that is not due to income insurance by looking at the dividend-share orthogonalized with respect to the labor-share. Even if the negative correlation between the dividend-share and the labor-share is highly significant, the latter explains only about 8% of the variation in the former over the 1996:4 to 2010:1 period, for which we have dividend strip returns. Thus, a nonnegligible component of transitory dividend risk should be imputed to sources different from income insurance (such as financial or investment decisions as well as the dividend policy itself).
Second, I use the orthogonalized dividend-share as a control in the regressions of dividends' VRs at several horizons on the labor-share. If transitory risk distinct from income insurance drives the timing of dividend risk, we should find a significant coefficient on the orthogonalized dividend-share as well as an increase in the adjusted-R2. Panel A of TableVreports the results. The coefficient on the orthogonalized dividend-share is never significant and its sign changes with the horizon. This suggests that transitory risk distinct from income insurance does not help explain the timing of dividend risk. Indeed, the adjusted-R2s are almost identical to those in Panel C of TableI.
Third, I use the orthogonalized dividend-share as a control in the regressions of dividend strip return volatility, excess return over the risk-free rate, and excess return over the market return on the current labor-share. Panel B of TableVreports the results. The coefficient on the orthogonalized dividend-share is not significant in eight out of the nine regressions, and the adjusted-R2s are almost identical to those of the univariate regressions in TableII.
In sum, the results in this section show that even if transitory dividend risk distinct from income insurance is nonnegligible, it does not help explain the timing of dividend risk, short-term equity risk, premium, or slope. This is not surprising from inspection of the right panel of Figure1: the decreasing VRs of output minus wages almost recover the negative slope of dividends. Thus, even if alternative mechanisms to income insurance could potentially affect the timing of dividend risk, it seems they do not.18These results provide further support for the term structure effect of income insurance.
article.II. Model:
A representative firm produces operational cash flows, which can be interpreted as total output minus investments:C=Y−I. Such operational cash flows represent the total resources shared by workers and shareholders: the former receive wages (W) and the latter receive dividends (D). The resource constraint requires thatC=W+D. To keep the model simple, I assume limited market participation such that workers do not access the financial markets but instead consume their wages. Consequently, shareholders act as a representative agent on the stock market and consume dividends.19
The standard Walrasian Cobb-Douglas economy leads to a constant labor-share and workers paid at their marginal productivity:Wt=αCt, withα∈(0,1). The opposite extreme scenario would be the case in which workers are promised perfect income smoothing, that is, wages are constant (and potentially equal to the unconditional expected marginal productivity).
Preferences in equation1and the log-linearized dividend dynamics of equation10guarantee a model solution that emphasizes the role of the state variablesμtandztin the formation of prices. A first-order approximation of shareholders' consumption-wealth ratio around its endogenous steady state,ecq, provides closed-form solutions for prices and return moments up to such approximation (Benzoni, Collin-Dufresne, and Goldstein (2011)).
Although the state-price density equals shareholders' first-order condition, it depends on the risk attitudes of both agent types. The left-hand side of equation13is given by the exogenous flows of total resources produced by the firm. The right-hand side is given by the product of two terms: the optimality condition for market participants and a time-varying term that captures the distributional risk due to income insurance. The latter term equals the inverse of the dividend-share,Ct,/Dt, and its variability increases with ϕ. In the absence of income insurance, the labor-share is constant and the second term on the right-hand side of equation13reduces to(1−α)−1.20
Note that, even if the equilibrium state-price density is a function of the integrated processCt, the economy is characterized by a stationary equilibrium. This is a necessary condition to produce realistic testable implications. Many real business cycle models circumvent this problem by excluding permanent shocks (e.g.,Ct=Zt). Here, allowing for both permanent and transitory shocks (e.g.,Ct=XtZt) and still obtaining a stationary equilibrium is of crucial importance to study the equilibrium implications of the term structure of equity.
The permanent shock commands a price of riskθx(t)due to the contribution ofxtto the instantaneous volatility of shareholders' consumption. Long-run growth commands a price of riskθμ(t), which has the usual form in long-run risk models. This is a price for the contribution ofμtto the variation in shareholders' continuation utility value. Hence,θμ(t)disappears under power utility (ψ→γ−1). Such a price of risk increases with the preference for the early resolution of uncertaintyγ−1/ψand decreases with the rate of reversionλμ. The transitory shock leads to a price of riskθz(t), which has two components. The first,γdzσz, is a positive price for the contribution ofztto the instantaneous volatility of shareholders' consumption. The second,θz(t)−γdzσz, is a price for the contribution ofztto the variation in shareholders' continuation utility value. At the opposite ofθμ, this term is negative and increasing in the rate of reversionλzunder a preference for the early resolution of uncertainty. This term disappears under power utility. Interestingly, both components ofθz(t)are proportional to the coefficientdz>1, which is increasing in ϕ. Therefore, forγ>ψ>1, income insurance leads to a positive price for its effect on current dividend volatility and a negative price for its effect on the evolution of shareholders' utility.
The slope of the term structure of volatility depends on two terms due toμtandzt. The first is always positive and hence implies an upward-sloping effect. The second term is negative if the intertemporal substitution effect dominates the wealth effect and vice versa. Therefore, the term structure of volatility is monotone upward-sloping ifψ<1, whereas it is not necessarily monotone ifψ>1. A nonmonotone (e.g., U-shaped) term structure of risk obtains if the leverage effect due to income insurance,dz, outweighs the upward-sloping effect due to long-run growth for some horizons τ.
Such an analytical result explains why the standard long-run risk model cannot capture recent evidence about dividend strips. Long-run risk models (i.e.,Ct=Xt) rule out transitory shockztand do a good job at matching a number of asset pricing moments as long asψ>1. The term structures of volatility and premia are both monotone increasing. The alternative scenarios, whereby either the transitory shock enters the model (Ct=Zt) or long-run growth is constant (Ct=XtZtwithμt=μ¯), lead to monotone decreasing term structures of risk and premia forψ>1. However, in such cases, the equity premium is not sizeable since it decreases with ψ. Instead, when we account for bothμtandzt, the model can accommodate both a high equity premium and downward-sloping term structures of equity risk and premia in the short run. Therefore, the dynamics of dividends induced by income insurance reconcile the above stylized facts.
The equity premium is given by three components due to the three shocks. The permanent shockxtrequires the usual positive compensationγσx2. Long-run growth leads to a premium that is positive if the intertemporal substitution effect dominates the wealth effect and shareholders have a preference for the early resolution of uncertainty. Instead,ztcommands a premium that is always positive, decreasing with ψ, and increasing with γ. Furthermore, this compensation term is increasing in the degree of income insurance.
article.III. Asset Pricing Results:
Model parameters are set by choosing cash flow parameters to match key moments of the time series of consumption, wage, and dividend growth rates and by choosing preference parameters to provide a good fit of standard asset pricing moments.
The left panel of Figure4plots the model-implied term structures of VRs for aggregate consumption, wages, and dividends, as well as their empirical counterparts. The model accurately captures the increasing (decreasing) relation between wage (dividend) risk and the horizon. Therefore, the calibration procedure does a good job at recovering the shape of the empirical term structures and hence the timing of consumption, wage, and dividend risk.
The right panel of Figure4shows that the empirical and model-implied autocorrelation functions of the labor-share are very similar. This is interesting because, even if autocorrelations do not enter the calibration procedure, the ability of the model to match the gap in the VRs of wages and dividends leads to an accurate description of the persistence of the main model's state variable (the half-life is about 3.5 years).
The decline in the timing of dividend risk is due to the levered exposure of dividends to the transitory component of consumption,zt. The operating leverage coefficient,dz=1+ϕα1−α=6.5, allows for both (i) the correct slope of the term structure of dividend volatility and (ii) the excess volatility of dividends over consumption in yearly growth rates (i.e., 17.4% versus 2.5%). At the same time, the model leads to smooth wage growth rates (i.e., 1.7%).
SectionI.empirically investigates the term structure effect of income insurance by recognizing a dynamic relationship between the labor-share and both the VRs of dividends and the differential in the VRs of wages and dividends. The former relationship is negative and the latter is positive, as documented in TableI. The model leads to the same result: the left and right panels of Figure5show that the income insurance mechanism drives heterogeneity in the timing of macroeconomic risk. When wages are high (low) relative to dividends, as a result of income insurance, dividend risk becomes more (less) downward-sloping and the gap between the timing of wage risk and dividend risk increases (decreases). Similarly, income insurance leads to dividend and consumption growth predictability. TableIVdocuments a positive intertemporal relationship between the current labor-share and future growth. The same result holds in the model, as shown in TableVIII. Since income insurance only concerns transitory risk, both the labor-share and expected growth are decreasing with the current level of the transitory shock. Hence, dividend and consumption growth predictability obtain.
A number of insights are worth noting. First, although very parsimonious, the model dynamics are flexible enough to capture the main properties of the empirical data. Thus, the calibration supports the model assumption of both permanent and transitory shocks. Second, income insurance leads to heterogeneity in the term structures of the risk of consumption, wages, and dividends. Consistent with the empirical findings of SectionI., matching the positive slope of wage risk allows one to quantitatively fill the gap between the approximatively flat term structure of consumption risk and the downward-sloping term structure of dividend risk. Thus, the calibration further supports the idea that income insurance is the main determinant of the timing of macroeconomic risk. In the absence of income insurance (ϕ=0), the three term structures are equal and collapse on that of consumption. Third, the shape of the term structure of dividend risk is a result of the combination of a downward-sloping effect due toztand an upward-sloping effect due toμt. One issue with asset pricing models is that the main model mechanism often relies on a latent factor that is difficult to estimate. A calibration procedure that exploits the information implied by the term structures of the risk of macroeconomic variables (i) allows one to capture additional empirical moments, and (ii) offers a way to make inferences about the persistence (λμ,λz) and variance (σμ,σz) of the latent factors. Fourth, the long-run growth factorμthas moderately persistent and smooth dynamics (λμ=0.86andσμ=1.8%). The model does not require excessive persistence in expected consumption growth, as suggested by Constantinides and Ghosh (2011) and Beeler and Campbell (2012), but accurately captures the persistence of the labor-share.
Finally, I set shareholders' preference parameters such that they have a preference for the early resolution of uncertainty (γ>1/ψ) and the intertemporal substitution effect dominates the wealth effect (ψ>1), as in most of the asset pricing literature. Thus, in the baseline calibration, the pairγ=10andψ=1.5belongs to the usual range of values. The time-discount rate β is set to 3.5%.
In the baseline calibration (γ=10,ψ=1.5), the term structures of premia and return volatility on dividend strips are decreasing with the maturity at short and medium horizons—where the downward-sloping effect due to the transitory shock dominates the upward-sloping effect due to long-run growth—and approximatively flat at long horizons—where the two effects offset each other. The upper panels of Figure6depict these term structures.
The stronger the income insurance (ϕ), the larger the operating leverage effect on dividends (dz). This enhances the price associated with transitory risk and, forψ>1, induces a downward-sloping effect on the term structure of equity premia. The term structure is decreasing over a longer horizon and the slope is larger in magnitude.
The lower left panel of Figure6shows the term structure of Sharpe ratios. van Binsbergen and Koijen (2017) provide evidence that Sharpe ratios from dividend strip futures' returns are slightly increasing with maturity but are much larger than the Sharpe ratio of market returns in excess of the 10-year bond yield. Specifically, Sharpe ratios on futures from one to seven years' maturity range from 42% to 58% in the data (i.e., from 12% to 17% monthly), whereas the Sharpe ratio of the market return in excess of the 10-year bond is about 12.5% (i.e., 3.7% monthly). The corresponding model quantities have similar shapes: Sharpe ratios on futures from one to seven years' maturity range from 35% to 38.5%, whereas the Sharpe ratio of the market return in excess of a 10-year bond is about 7.7%. Thus, both empirical and model short-term Sharpe ratios are about five times larger than the market Sharpe ratio.24
The model generates a long-run excess volatility of equity over dividends in line with recent empirical evidence about the decreasing VRs of dividends documented by Belo, Collin-Dufresne, and Goldstein (2015) and in SectionI.. Instead, long-run risk models imply payouts to shareholders that are riskier than equity returns, as pointed out by Beeler and Campbell (2012). In particular, a long-run risk model (Ct=Xt) produces “long-run” excess volatility only forψ<1/2—a parameterization for which standard asset pricing implications fail to obtain.
The model reconciles evidence about the term structures of both equity and macroeconomic variables with standard asset pricing facts. The baseline calibration (γ=10,ψ=1.5) produces an unconditional equity premium of about 5.1%, which is quite close to the data. Such a result is remarkable since neither stochastic volatility and disasters nor unrealistically high and time-varying risk aversion are required. The return volatility is about 13.6%, which is somewhat lower than in the data. However, a slightly lowerψ=1.25increases return volatility to 15.8%. Moreover, return volatility leads to a large excess volatility over consumption and dividends on the entire term structure, including the long horizon. The model produces a Sharpe ratio (about 37.2%) that accurately matches the data. Such a result is peculiar and mostly due to transitory risk, which is amplified by income insurance. The model leads to a risk-free rate level (about 0.9%) and volatility (about 2.8%) that are very close to that in the data. The 20-year real bond yield is 3.6%, about 200 basis points higher than in TIPs data. The model also captures quite well the level and volatility of the price-dividend ratio (about exp (3.5) and 41.3%).
TableIXsummarizes the empirical and model-implied moments and reports the asset pricing moments for many pairs(γ,ψ). A low risk-free rate and high levels of the first two return moments of the market asset also obtain for (γ=10,ψ=1.25), (γ=7.5,ψ=1), and (γ=5,ψ=0.75). Decreasing the EIS leads to an increase in the equity premium but the price-dividend ratio is too smooth and the risk-free rate is too volatile in comparison with the data. Hence, the choice (γ=10,ψ=1.5) seems preferable. The operating leverage effect due to income insurance not only is crucial to the modeling of the term structures but also helps match the standard asset pricing moments (Danthine and Donaldson (2002)). The premium increases with ϕ and decreases with ψ, all else being equal. The latter relation has the following rationale. The correct calibration of the timing of macroeconomic risk implies that, in equilibrium, the price of transitory risk dominates the price of long-run variation in expected growth.
The case of no income insurance (ϕ=0), and hence constant labor-share, fails to describe both the risk-free rate and the equity premium puzzle as well as the downward-sloping term structure of equity.
To better understand the equilibrium equity premium, it is instructive to adopt a term structure perspective. The equity premium is a weighted average of dividend strip premia, where the weights are given by the ratio of the dividend strip price to the equity price. From the upper left panel of Figure6, we observe that dividend strip premia range from about 6.9% to 4.8%. Since the slope is negative but relatively tiny, a high equity premium (5.1%) can obtain.25The equilibrium densityH(t,τ)of equation31represents the relative contribution of each horizon τ to the entire premium. The horizon decomposition of the equity premium depends not only on the dynamics of the permanent and transitory shocks, but also on the equilibrium compensations for these risks. Figure7shows that, in the baseline calibration, the equilibrium density is monotone decreasing with the horizon. A stronger (weaker) income insurance mechanism leads to a shift of the density toward the short (long) horizon. Indeed, income insurance leads not only to riskier dividends but also to larger compensation for the transitory risk. This horizon decomposition sheds light on the nature of priced risk in equilibrium. A “short-run” explanation of market compensations is needed to simultaneously describe both the standard asset pricing moments and the term structures of fundamentals and equity.
Income insurance affects the equilibrium equity yields and their components: the yields on the riskless bond, expected dividend growth, and the premium on the equity yields, as defined by van Binsbergen et al. (2013). Figure8plots these quantities at the steady state as a function of the horizon both in the presence and in the absence of income insurance.
The left upper panel shows that the equity yield is decreasing with the horizon under income insurance, whereas it is increasing with the horizon in the absence of income insurance (ϕ=0). Forψ>1, the downward-sloping effect due to income insurance outweighs the upward-sloping effect due to long-run growth. The right upper panel shows the term structure of the real bond yields. In the absence of income insurance,μtdrives the term structure of interest rates. Similar to long-run risk models, a downward-sloping term structure obtains forψ>1, which is inconsistent with data from TIPs. In contrast, in the presence of income insurance,ztinduces a stronger upward-sloping effect, which leads to a positive slope for the bond yields. Intuitively, income insurance enhances transitory risk in equilibrium and hence bonds with short maturities are a better hedge than equity. Therefore, increasing bond yields obtain. Real yields (TIPs) are available for maturities of 5, 7, 10, and 20 years over the 2003 to 2015 sample period.


van Binsbergen and Koijen (2017) provide empirical evidence that forward equity yield volatility decreases with the horizon. Specially, for U.S. data over the 2002 to 2014 period, this volatility ranges from about 11% to 4% over maturities from one to seven years. The corresponding volatility in the baseline model calibration is also decreasing and ranges from about 15.7% to 5.2%.
In summary, the decomposition of equity yields provides two noteworthy results. First, income insurance helps explain the term structures of the equity yield premia, in line with the findings of van Binsbergen et al. (2013). Second, income insurance also helps reconcile the term structure of real interest rates with the equity results.
article.IV. Uncertainty and Time-Varying Slope o:
While downward-sloping dividend risk is a robust feature of the data, empirical evidence about the term structures of equity is subject to debate.26On the one hand, equity volatility inherits the negative slope of dividend risk; on the other hand, the slope of equity premia depends on how and how much the sources of long-run and short-run risks are priced in equilibrium. Downward-sloping equity premia documented by van Binsbergen, Brandt, and Koijen (2012) appear as an unconditional property of dividend strips' returns. Indeed, van Binsbergen et al. (2013) and Aït-Sahalia, Karaman, and Mancini (2015) provide evidence that the term structure of equity premia is time-varying and switches from flat or slightly increasing in good times to strongly decreasing in bad times.
This form of heteroskedasticity in fundamentals leads to two results. First, under income insurance, the wage-share is decreasing withztand hence dividends load more on the transitory shock when the latter is volatile, that is, operating leverage is countercyclical. Second, the price of risk associated with transitory risk is not constant but rather is decreasing withztand hence higher compensations obtain during business cycle downturns. These two facts lead to a slope of the term structure of equity premia that is time-varying. Long-run growth leads to an upward-sloping effect and the transitory shock leads to a downward-sloping effect, but the latter depends on the volatility ofzt. Therefore, the downward-sloping effect is counter-cyclical. As a result, the term structure of equity premia can be flat or slightly upward-sloping in good times and strongly downward-sloping in bad times.
To illustrate the equilibrium results, I calibrate the model using the same procedure as in the previous section. Forβ=4.5%,γ=7.5, andψ=1.5, the model generates steady-state moments in line with the data: a risk-free rate of 1.1% with volatility of 1.7%, an equity premium of 6.0% with return volatility of 14.5% and Sharpe ratio of 41.5%, and log price-dividend ratio of 3.20 with volatility of 41.5%.
The model of this section also leads to time variation in the equity premium. The premium on the market asset is a decreasing function of the transitory shock forγ>ψ>1. A common way to assess the time-varying properties of risk premia is to verify whether the model captures the pattern of predictability observed in actual data. Accordingly, I regress long-horizon excess returns on the log price-dividend ratio. The regression is performed on returns measured over horizons ranging from one to seven years. In each simulation, the economy is simulated at the monthly frequency over a 67-year horizon. Panel A of TableXshows that the model reproduces the negative and statistically significant intertemporal relationship between the current valuation ratios and future excess returns that we observe in the data (Cochrane (2008), Lettau and van Nieuwerburgh (2008), van Binsbergen and Koijen (2010)). Moreover, the explanatory power and statistical significance increase with the horizon. Panel B shows that the price-dividend ratio also predicts consumption growth. However, the explanatory power is much smaller and inconsistent with the data.27
Overall, income insurance, combined with business cycle uncertainty, helps to jointly explain the timing of dividend risk, the dynamic slope of the term structure of equity, the main properties of the market asset and risk-free rate, and the long-horizon predictability of equity excess returns.
article.V. Conclusion:
This paper documents that the timing of the risk of output, wages, and dividends is heterogeneous. Income insurance from shareholders to workers, which concerns only transitory risk, empirically and theoretically explains these term structures and provides a rationale for downward-sloping dividend risk. Once the resulting dividend dynamics are embedded in an otherwise standard general equilibrium asset pricing model, the negative slope of dividend risk transmits to equity returns under standard preferences. Thus, income insurance allows one to reconcile in equilibrium traditional asset pricing facts—such as the risk-free rate and equity premium puzzle, the excess volatility of equity, the increasing real yields on bonds, and long-horizon equity predictability—with the new evidence on the term structures of both fundamentals and equity and their dynamics. As a result, labor-share variation well forecasts dividend strips' risk, premium, and slope.
The model can be extended in a number of directions to shed light on, for example, interest rates and cross-sectional equity returns. Marfè (2015a,2015b) empirically and theoretically support the idea that the term structure effect of income insurance helps explain the joint term structures of real and nominal interest rates and the dynamics of the value premium.
Editor: Kenneth J. Singleton
